retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Platelet Refractoriness
retrieved_docs.content: Platelet refractoriness occurs when posttransfusion platelet recovery is not as expected. The condition may arise from various causes, ranging from immune to nonimmune, although a multi-transfused patient often develops antibodies against specific human platelets or human leukocyte antigens. The antibodies trigger the immune system to destroy donor platelets. Platelet crossmatching can reduce platelet refractoriness. Human leukocyte antigen–matched platelets or a matching human platelet antigen is recommended, especially if an anti-human platelet antibody is involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Nonimmune-mediated febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction arises from exposure to white blood cell cytokines accumulating during product storage. [22] Biological response modifiers such as sCD40L, interleukin-6 (IL-6), IL-7, IL-8, IL-27, and transforming growth factor-β may be released during platelet storage. Elevated biological response modifier concentrations are typically observed after prolonged storage, particularly from the third  day onward, potentially correlating with recipient adverse events. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Transfusion-Related Immunomodulation
retrieved_docs.content: Leukocytes and biological response modifiers released during storage have been implicated in immunomodulation. The condition leads to a modified response of T-helper cell types 1 and 2, predisposing the patient to infection and delayed postsurgical recovery. Transfusion-related immunomodulation may also be prevented by prestorage leukoreduction and depleting plasma from platelets. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Delayed Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: As with acute reactions, clinicians divide delayed reactions into immune- and nonimmune-mediated reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Alloimmunization
retrieved_docs.content: Alloimmunization occurs when the immune system becomes sensitized to antigens not present in the host. This can lead to the production of alloantibodies against RBC-specific antigens, human leukocyte antigen (HLA) I antigens on platelets and leukocytes, HLA II antigens present on leukocytes, granulocyte-specific antigens, and platelet-specific or human platelet antigens (HPA).

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Immune-mediated febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction results from endogenous pyrogen release from the donor or recipient's white blood cells due to a reaction between donor antigens and recipient antibodies. [24] Antibodies targeting human leukocyte antigens or leukocyte antigens in donor plasma are most commonly implicated. This interaction leads to donor white cell antigens binding with recipient antibodies, triggering the release of pyrogens and cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6.

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
retrieved_docs.content: AHTRs can occur due to immune or nonimmune-related red blood cell (RBC) hemolysis. This section will focus on immune-mediated HTR due to immunologic incompatibility between the donor and recipient RBCs. Immune-mediated-hemolysis can be due to ABO or non-ABO antibodies. These antibodies can be immunoglobulin M (IgM), which activates complement, causing intravascular hemolysis, or IgG, which mainly causes extravascular hemolysis, or both. In most cases, hemolysis due to ABO incompatibility is due to a clerical error. [13] Non-ABO hemolysis is multi-factorial. Most HTRs are due to RBC transfusion; however, less commonly, transfusion of plasma and platelet products containing high titers of isoagglutinin can also cause hemolysis of recipient RBCs. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Selective IgA Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: To date, researchers have not demonstrated IgG-dependent anaphylaxis in humans. Based on animal models, proposed mechanisms of IgG-mediated anaphylactic reactions suggest that the allergen interacts with allergen-specific IgG bound to Fc-γ-RIII on macrophages and basophils. The macrophage activation results in the release of platelet-activating factor rather than histamine. ΡAF causes platelets to aggregate and release thromboxane A2 and serotonin, which cause vasoconstriction and increase vascular permeability. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Mild allergic: Attributed to hypersensitivity to a foreign protein in the donor product.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Disseminated intravascular coagulation Lung injury Renal failure Hemolysis Death

retrieved_docs.title: Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for the first hit include shock, liver surgery, current smoking, chronic alcohol abuse, higher peak airway pressure, positive fluid balance, low IL-10 levels, and systemic inflammation. In cases of TRALI, elevated C-reactive protein levels are often observed, reflecting an inflammatory response. Transient leukopenia, a temporary decrease in white blood cell count, may also be part of the immune reaction. Additionally, donor antibodies against human leukocyte antigens (HLA) and human neutrophil antigens (HNA) are commonly implicated in TRALI, contributing to immune-mediated lung injury. Nonantibody-mediated TRALI occurs when no detectable anti-HLA or HNA antibodies are present, but the reaction is still triggered by other unidentified antigens or biological reactive molecules (BRM). [10] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Noninfectious Complications of Blood Transfusion -- Issues of Concern -- Alloimmunization
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen-matched transfusions would effectively prevent alloimmunization. To do so, the patient's ABO, Rh, Kell, Kidd, and Duffy systems should be typed at diagnosis or before the institution of transfusion therapy. Blood should always be matched, at least with the ABO, Rh, and Kell systems.[ 2 ] Further, leukoreduction helps prevent alloimmunization. [24] Patients who have experienced alloimmunization should receive crossmatch-compatible antigen-negative RBCs. Those who experience refractoriness to platelet transfusions should avoid future platelet transfusions as much as possible. When necessary, give ABO-compatible apheresis platelets less than 48 hours old. Consider using donor platelets from family members or select HLA-matched and crossmatched platelets. Donor platelets from family members must be irradiated to prevent graft-versus-host disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Immune Thrombocytopenia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Posttransfusion purpura is a transfusion-associated reaction occurring about a week after the transfusion. The condition is an amnestic response to platelet antigens, destroying native and transfused platelets. [46] Posttransfusion purpura may occur in multiparous women due to alloimmunization against platelet antigens. [47] This disorder is treated with IVIG.

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Allergic and Anaphylactic Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: The reported incidence of anaphylactic reactions is 1 in 30,000 to 1 in 42,000 per unit of blood product transfused. The condition may occur in patients with immunoglobulin A (IgA) deficiency, as anti-IgA antibodies target IgA-containing platelets. Anaphylactic transfusion reactions develop rapidly. Patients present with hypotension, shock, angioedema, and bronchospasm, leading to respiratory distress and wheezing. Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency requiring prompt evaluation and treatment. This transfusion reaction may be prevented by administering platelets that are washed or obtained from IgA-deficient donors. [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Platelet Transfusion -- Complications -- Febrile Nonhemolytic Transfusion Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction is a diagnosis of exclusion, determined based on specific characteristics and by excluding other causes of fever through additional investigations and laboratory findings. Febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction risk can be reduced by 50% through prestorage leukodepletion. [25] Platelets stored in platelet additive solution replace most of the plasma, reducing allergen content and the likelihood of reactions. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Reactions -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Transfusion-related acute lung injury: Acute lung injury is due to antibodies in the donor product (human leukocyte antigen or human neutrophil antigen) reacting with antigens in the recipient. The recipient’s immune system responds and causes the release of mediators that lead to pulmonary edema. Possibly contributing to this are clinical conditions that predispose the patient including infection, recent surgery, or inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1761
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is classified as a type of تسريب, which itself is a kind of نقل الدم (blood transfusion) and belongs to the broader category of الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (therapeutic procedures not classified elsewhere). In addition to its relationship with تسريب, INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is also linked to فصادة الكريات البيض (white‑blood‑cell apheresis) and to نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), indicating that it shares characteristics with both apheresis procedures and self‑donated blood transfusion. نقل الدم encompasses نقل الدم الذاتي as a specific child category, and the term blood transfusion is used to classify نقل الدم. Blood transfusion is associated with complications of surgical procedures or medical care, highlighting that transfusion practices can be a cause of such complications. Together, these relationships illustrate how various procedural categories—INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS, تسريب, فصادة الكريات البيض, and نقل الدم الذاتي—are interconnected within the overarching framework of blood‑related therapeutic interventions and their potential impact on patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1873
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of surgical procedures or medical care are considered a subcategory of تفاعل نقل الدم, indicating that these postoperative and procedural complications fall under the broader concept of blood transfusion reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 421
retrieved_docs.content: Blood transfusion, known in Arabic as نقل الدم, is a medical procedure used to replace lost blood components and is classified under the broader concept of blood replacement therapy. It encompasses specific subtypes, including نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), which involves collecting and reinfusing the patient’s own blood. Although blood transfusion is a valuable therapeutic intervention, it can also be a cause of complications related to surgical procedures or other medical care, underscoring the importance of careful indication, thorough screening, and vigilant monitoring to minimize adverse outcomes. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship between general blood transfusion, its child category autologous transfusion, and the potential for procedural complications is essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2183
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). This specific immunoprophylactic intervention provides immediate, short‑term protection by supplying pre‑formed antibodies, thereby preventing Rh sensitization in Rh‑negative patients exposed to Rh‑positive blood. As a passive immunization strategy, the injection of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how targeted antibody administration can be used to avert alloimmunization and its associated complications in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: INJECTIONS AND IMPLANTATIONS is positioned within both فصادة الكريات البيض (leukapheresis) and نقل الدم الذاتي (autologous blood transfusion), indicating that injection and implantation techniques are considered relevant to white‑blood‑cell filtration procedures as well as to the practice of using a patient’s own blood for transfusion. نقل الدم (blood transfusion) is classified as “blood transfusion” and serves as the parent category for نقل الدم الذاتي, establishing autologous blood transfusion as a specific subtype of the broader transfusion process. Physical Treatment Methods are also linked as a child of نقل الدم, suggesting that certain physical therapeutic approaches are conceptually grouped under the umbrella of blood transfusion practices. Importantly, نقل الدم is recognized as a potential cause of complications of surgical procedures or medical care, underscoring the need for careful risk assessment and management when transfusion is employed. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical network in which injection/implantation procedures, leukapheresis, autologous transfusion, and related physical treatments are interconnected within the larger context of blood transfusion and its clinical implications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.
